content.problemStatement:
You are given a permutation†^\dagger† PPP of the integers 111 to NNN.

You can perform the following three-step operation on it as many times as you like (possibly, zero):


First, choose any subset SSS of indices of PPP, whose size is between 111 and N−1N-1N−1. (That is, SSS should not be the empty set or the entirety of {1,2,…,N}\{1, 2, \ldots, N\}{1,2,…,N}.)

Note that SSS is a subset, so it need not consist of contiguous elements.
Then, paint all the elements at indices of SSS red, and all the elements at indices that are not in SSS blue.
Finally, place all the red elements at indices of SSS in **ascending order**, and blue elements at indices outside SSS in **descending order.**
Performing this operation costs **one coin.**

For example, suppose P=[1,3,4,2,5]P = [1, 3, 4, 2, 5]P=[1,3,4,2,5]. Suppose you choose the **set of indices** {2,4,5}\{2, 4, 5\}{2,4,5}. Then,


Indices 2,4,52, 4, 52,4,5 are painted red, and indices 1,31, 31,3 are painted blue.
Red elements (P2=3P_2 = 3P2​=3 and P4=2,P5=5P_4 = 2, P_5 = 5P4​=2,P5​=5) are sorted in ascending order at placed at indices 2,4,52, 4, 52,4,5, while blue elements (P1=1,P3=4P_1 = 1, P_3 = 4P1​=1,P3​=4) are sorted in descending order and placed at indices 1,31, 31,3.
This turns PPP into [4,2,1,3,5][4, 2, 1, 3, 5][4,2,1,3,5].
After performing all the operations you wish to perform, you will receive one coin for every index iii such that Pi=iP_i = iPi​=i.

What's the **maximum** profit you can earn, if you perform operations optimally?

Here, profit means the number of coins you receive, minus the number of coins you spent on operations.

†^\dagger† A permutation of the integers 111 to NNN is an array of length NNN containing each integer from 111 to NNN exactly once. For example, if N=3N = 3N=3, [2,1,3][2, 1, 3][2,1,3] and [3,2,1][3, 2, 1][3,2,1] are permutations, but [2,1,4][2, 1, 4][2,1,4] and [1,2,2][1, 2, 2][1,2,2] are not.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN — the size of the permutation.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers P1,P2,…,PNP_1, P_2, \ldots, P_NP1​,P2​,…,PN​.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the maximum number of coins you can obtain as profit.
constraints: The sum of NNN over all test cases won't exceed 3⋅1053\cdot 10^53⋅105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: We don't need to perform any operation: already P1=1P_1 = 1P1​=1 and P2=2P_2 = 2P2​=2 will hold, so we receive two coins and don't pay anything, for a total profit of 222.
Test case 222: It's optimal to not perform any operation, we'll receive 000 coins but also pay 000, for a net profit of 000.
Test case 333: Choose S={1,2,3,4}S = \{1, 2, 3, 4\}S={1,2,3,4}. Then,

The elements {P1,P2,P3,P4}\{P_1, P_2, P_3, P_4\}{P1​,P2​,P3​,P4​} will be placed in ascending order at indices 1,2,3,41, 2, 3, 41,2,3,4.
The element P5P_5P5​ will be placed at index 555.
The resulting permutation is P=[1,2,3,4,5]P = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]P=[1,2,3,4,5].

We receive 555 coins for this, and paid one - so net profit of 5−1=45 - 1 = 45−1=4. This is optimal.
